docs.text: DSP-METADATA-GUI Metadata Module The dsp-metadata-gui is a GUI application written in Python for collecting project specific metadata and turn it into RDF. As part of the dsp-tools , its aim is to enable researchers and project managers who deposit research data on the DaSCH Service Platform (DSP), to add metadata about the project and datasets to the DSP repository. By providing metadata, the project will be searchable on the platform, which is an integral part of the FAIR principles. The metadata follows the schema defined by the dsp-ontologies . Install and run The module provides a command line entry point to run the GUI. The only requirement is Python 3 and PIP. The application has only been tested on Python 3.9, but it might work on older versions too. Note: There have been issues with conda installations. If this is the case, consider using a virtual environment. Installation via pip To install the application, run: pip install dsp-metadata-gui Or respectively: pip3 install dsp-metadata-gui Afterwards, the program can be started by running the command dsp-metadata in your terminal of choice. Installation from source Clone this repo and run make make-and-run . If you don't use GNU Make, run the commands specified in the Makefile manually. This will package the application, install it to your python environment and run the application. Usage The application is divided into two windows: The main window lets you organize a list of projects, for which you can collect metadata. Several actions can be performed with projects, e.g. editing or exporting the project. When editing a project, in the project window, the actual metadata can be added, modified and saved. To add a project, you will need the project short code, which is assigned to you by the DaSCH Client Services. A project is always associated with a folder on your local machine. If any files should be included with the metadata import, these files must be within that folder. Once all metadata are added and valid, and the overall RDF graph of the metadata set validates against the ontology, the project can be exported for upload to the DSP. All data is locally stored in the file ~/DaSCH/config/repos.data . for more detail, see here . Development Development Environment Pipenv Use pipenv for a seamless development experience. In order to have both dependencies and dev-dependencies installed from the Pipfile , set up the virtual environment running pipenv instal --dev pipenv will manage dependencies as well as a virtual environment. To install packages, use pipenv install <package-name> To create requirements.txt , run pipenv lock -r > requirements.txt To bring setup.py up to date, run pipenv run pipenv-setup sync GNU Make GNU Make is used to automatize most tasks. Run make help for info on the available targets. Note: All make targets - except make run - should be run from within the pipenv shell: Either by running pipenv run make <target-name> or by opening a virtual pipenv shell by running pipenv shell make <target-name> ... exit Documentation The documentation is created using mkdocs and mkdocstrings with markdown_include.include . To create the documentation, make sure to install all of these, using pip. To serve the documentation locally, run make doc . To deploy the documentation to github pages, run make deploy-doc .
docs.title: Overview

docs.text: The dsp-metadata-gui is a GUI application written in Python for collecting project specific metadata and turn it into RDF. As part of the dsp-tools , its aim is to enable researchers and project managers who deposit research data on the DaSCH Service Platform (DSP), to add metadata about the project and datasets to the DSP repository. By providing metadata, the project will be searchable on the platform, which is an integral part of the FAIR principles. The metadata follows the schema defined by the dsp-ontologies .

docs.title: Documentation

docs.text: Dataset This class represents one (of potentially many) dataset of the project. Is Part of Links the dataset to its project. Note: This property is read-only and can not be changed. Title The title of the dataset. (Mandatory.) Abstract Abstract of the dataset. Can be the abstract text itself or a link to an abstract online. (Mandatory. Can have multiple values.) Language The language the dataset is primarily written in. (Mandatory. Can have multiple values.) Type of Data The type of data the dataset is primarily made up of. Select values from the drop-down. The available options are: XML, Text, Image, Movie, Audio. (Mandatory. Can have multiple values.) Qualified Attribution Attribution of contributions to the dataset by people or organizations. A qualified attribution consists of a sub-property agent denoting the person or organization (select from the drop-down), and a sub-property role , denoting the role the person/organization had, within the project. (Mandatory. Can have multiple values.) License The license terms under which the dataset is published. Note: A URL to the license definition is expected. (Mandatory. Can have multiple values.) How to Cite Recommendation, how the dataset should be cited. (Mandatory.) Conditions of Access Access conditions of the dataset. Should normally be open access. A temporary embargo could be mentioned here too. (Mandatory.) Status The status of the dataset within a project life cycle. Select values from the drop-down. The available options are: In planning, Ongoing, On hold, Finished. (Mandatory.) Alternative URL Alternative URL of the dataset. This could for example include a link to a project specific website. (Optional.) Documentation Documentation of the dataset. This can either be a documentation text or a link to an online documentation. (Optional. Can have multiple values.) Date Published The publication date of the dataset. Use the date picker to select a date. (Optional.) Date Created The date when the dataset was first collected. Use the date picker to select a date. (Optional.) Date Modified The date when the dataset was last changed. Use the date picker to select a date. (Optional.) Distribution Link to a download of the entire dataset. (Optional.)
docs.title: Dataset

docs.text: Grant This class represents financing granted to the project by a person or organization. Funder The funding person or organization. Select a person or organization from the drop-down menu. (Mandatory. Can have multiple values.) Name The name of the grant, if any (e.g. "Ambizione", "Eccellenza", etc.). (Optional.) Number The number associated with the grant. (Optional.) URL The URL of the grant. (Optional.)

docs.text: Project Organization The project organization window consists of three sections: On the top, there is a list of all projects. If there are none, you will need to add one; otherwise the buttons are mostly greyed out. On the bottom left, there is a preview of the RDF representation of the selected project. On the bottom right, there are the buttons for the available actions to perform. All buttons, except adding a new project, require a project to be selected. If not, they will be greyed out. Graphical User Interface Project List The project list shows the available projects. For each projects, it displays the associated folder, the project name, shortcode and a list of associated files. It also gives indication of the status, i.e. validity of the project and the number of missing properties. RDF Preview The RDF preview displays a turtle serialization of the metadata of the selected project. Actions The following actions can be performed: Add new Project Adds a new project to the list. To be able to add a project, you need to know its shortcode as well as associate a folder on your local file system with it. For details, see below. Import Project Lets you import a project from binaries (see "Zip and Export") and add it to the list. Remove selected Project Removes the selected project from the list. Note: This can not be undone. Edit selected Project Opens the form for collecting metadata . Validate selected Project Validates the selected project against the ontology . Export selected Project as RDF Exports the selected project metadata as Turtle, RDF/XML and JSON-LD. The files are stored in the folder associated with the project. Zip and Export Project This action creates a Zip archive, containing all the files associated with the project, RDF serializations of the metadata, and a binary serialization of the project for reimport into this tool. Concepts Project Shortcode The project shortcode is a four digit hexadecimal number that is assigned by the DaSCH to each project on the DSP. To be able to add a project to the list, you need to enter the shortcode. The shortcode can not be altered after the project has been created. Be sure to have the shortcode ready at hand when you want to add a new project. If you do not have a a shortcode for your project yet, please get in touch with the DaSCH Client Services . Folder and Files Folder When adding a new project, after entering the project shortcode, you need to select a folder of your local file system, that is associated with the project. Note: Do not move or remove that folder, it might cause you to lose all data related to this project - or in the worst case, cause the tool to crash. This folder must hold all files you want to associate with the project. It is also where the RDF is exported to. Files If you not only want to submit project metadata, but also primary project data with it (e.g. like this is done on Zenodo), this data should come in the simplest possible form. Ideally, all data should be combined into one ZIP archive. When creating a project and associating the folder with it, all files in this folder are automatically associated with the project. However, files can be added or removed at any time in the project base data , provided that the files are in the folder . Local Data Storage The metadata tool stores all data (except data you export) as binary data in the file ~/DaSCH/config/repos.data . If you want to back up your working data, be sure to make backups of this file as well as all folders you associated with projects. Deleting this file will remove all projects and cause irreversible loss of all data you have entered. When the tool has a major update with breaking changes to the API, you will need to delete this file manually.
docs.title: Project Organization

docs.text: Organization This class represents an organization or legal body. Legal Name The name of the organization. (Mandatory. Can have multiple values.) E-Mail The contact e-mail address of the organization. (Optional.) URL The website of the organization. (Optional.) Address The postal address of the organization. (Optional.) Consists of three sub-properties: Street, postal code, locality.
docs.title: Organization

docs.text: Person This class represents a person. Family Name The family name of the person. (Mandatory.) Note: One value only. If multiple are required, use semicolon to separate them. Given Name The given name of the person. (Mandatory. Can have multiple values.) Member of The affiliation of the person. Select an organization from the drop-down menu. (Mandatory. Can have multiple values.) Job Title The job title or position of the person in the context of the project. (Mandatory. Can have multiple values.) E-Mail The contact e-mail address of the person. (Optional.) Alternative URL Entry in an authority file. (e.g. ORCID, VIAF, GND) (Optional. Can have multiple values.) Address The postal address of the person. (Optional.) Consists of three sub-properties: Street, postal code, locality.
docs.title: Person

docs.text: Project Tab This class represents the project itself. Name The official project name. (Mandatory.) Shortcode The unique identifier assigned to the project by the DaSCH Client Services. This needs to be entered upon project creation and can not be changed later on. The property is read-only. URL The project website. This should always be the DSP ARK URL (which will be provided by the DaSCH Client Services). Optionally, a second URL can be entered, e.g. pointing to a project-specific website. (Mandatory. Second value optional.) Description Description of the project. This should be the description text rather than a URL. (Mandatory.) Keywords Keywords/Tags describing the project. (Mandatory. Can have multiple values.) Discipline The scholarly discipline of the project. This should either be a link to the UNESCO nomenclature for fields of science and technology or a term from the list provided by the SNSF . (Mandatory. Can have multiple values.) Start Date Start date of the project. Use the date picker to select a date. (Mandatory.) End Date End date of the project. Use the date picker to select a date. (Optional.) Temporal Coverage The temporal coverage of the project. Should be a link to a term in Periodo or Chronontology . (Mandatory. Can have multiple values.) Spatial Coverage The spatial coverage of the project. Should be a link to a term in Geonames or Pleiades . (Mandatory. Can have multiple values.) Funder The funder of the project. Select a person or organization from the drop-down. (Mandatory. Can have multiple values.) Grant The grant by which the project is financed. Select a grant from the drop-down. (Optional. Can have multiple values.) Alternate Name Alternative name of the project, e.g. if the project is typically referred to by a short name. (Optional. Can have multiple values.) Data Management Plan Data management plan of the project. Use the checkbox to indicate if there is a data management plan at all. Add a link to the data management plan, if available online. (Optional.) Publications Publications published during the lifetime of the project. (Optional.) Contact Point Designated contact point for the project. Use the drop-down to select a person or organization. (Optional.)
docs.title: Project

docs.text: Entering Metadata When you select to edit a project, a new window opens. This window allows you to enter and modify the project metadata. It is divided in a number of tabs. At any given time, you can save the data you entered and close the window with or without saving, using the buttons on the bottom of the window. Base Data Tab The first tab shows the project's base data. Here, you can associate or dissociate files with the project. (For details, see here .) Metadata Tabs All other tabs each correspond to a top level class in the metadata, and share the same general layout. There is a row for each property. The value(s) of the property can be edited in the middle. Furthermore, there are two buttons to show further information on the property: The button labeled "?" explains what is meant by the property, i.e. what kind of value is expected. If available, there is also a simple, imaginary example. The button labeled either "Mandatory" or "Optional" gives validation information on the current value. The colors indicate, wether or not the value is valid (green = valid; red = invalid; gray = no value entered for optional property). For some more information why the value is (in)valid, click the button. Classes/Tabs The metadata consist of instances of classes. Each complex class has a corresponding tab in the GUI, where instances of the class can be organized and edited. Classes have properties, that can either be literal values (e.g. strings, URLs, etc.) or references to instances of other classes (e.g. a dataset being belonging to a project). Organization There can be multiple organizations. This class represents an organization that contributed to the project. Person There can be multiple persons. This class represents a person that contributed to the project. Grant There can be multiple grants. This class represents a financial grant that was granted to the project. Dataset There can be multiple datasets. A project hosted on the DSP must have at least one dataset. Project The project is the only class of which there can only be one, in the metadata. The tab therefor does not have the list of multiple instances. This class holds all information that is representative of the entire project. Properties, Cardinalities, Values Multiple Instances of a Class All classes, except the project, can have multiple instances. E.g. there will be multiple people contributing to a project. Where this is the case, the tab shows a list at the very top, representing all instances of the class. Additional instances can be added and existing instances can be removed with the buttons next to it. Once there are multiple instances, select the one you want to work on in the list. You can only edit properties of the instance that is currently selected. Cardinalities Each property of a class has a defined cardinality, as defined in the ontology. Generally, a property can either be optional or mandatory . Furthermore, a property can either be restricted to occur only once, or it can potentially occur multiple times. (Combined, several cardinalities are possible, e.g. 1 , 0-1 , 0-n , 1-n but also 1-2 etc.) Except for some cases that should be self-explanatory, the GUI mostly differentiates between properties that can occur maximum one time, and properties that can occur multiple times. Where only one value is expected, there will only be an input field. Where multiple values can occur, there is an input field, a list of all values and buttons to add and remove values to/from the list. Note: A value is only added to the list, once the "+" button is pressed or enter is hit. For an example, see the image below: While a dataset can only have one title, it may consist of multiple languages. Values Generally, the value of a property can either be a text-like value (e.g. Text, URLs, etc.), or it can link another object (Dataset, Project, Person, etc.). Text-like values are represented as text fields in the GUI. The value can simply be typed in. Object links are represented as dropdown lists in the GUI. The dropdown shows all objects that can be referenced in this place. The linkable objects can be created and modified in their respective tab. Example: A Project can have either a person or an organization as a point of contact.
docs.title: Entering Metadata

docs.title: Metadata Tabs

docs.text: The metadata consist of instances of classes. Each complex class has a corresponding tab in the GUI, where instances of the class can be organized and edited. Classes have properties, that can either be literal values (e.g. strings, URLs, etc.) or references to instances of other classes (e.g. a dataset being belonging to a project).
docs.title: Classes/Tabs

docs.text: There can be multiple organizations. This class represents an organization that contributed to the project.
docs.title: Organization

docs.text: There can be multiple persons. This class represents a person that contributed to the project.
docs.title: Person

docs.text: There can be multiple grants. This class represents a financial grant that was granted to the project.
docs.title: Grant

docs.text: There can be multiple datasets. A project hosted on the DSP must have at least one dataset.
docs.title: Dataset

docs.text: The project is the only class of which there can only be one, in the metadata. The tab therefor does not have the list of multiple instances. This class holds all information that is representative of the entire project.
docs.title: Project

docs.text: All classes, except the project, can have multiple instances. E.g. there will be multiple people contributing to a project. Where this is the case, the tab shows a list at the very top, representing all instances of the class. Additional instances can be added and existing instances can be removed with the buttons next to it. Once there are multiple instances, select the one you want to work on in the list. You can only edit properties of the instance that is currently selected.
docs.title: Multiple Instances of a Class

docs.text: Each property of a class has a defined cardinality, as defined in the ontology. Generally, a property can either be optional or mandatory . Furthermore, a property can either be restricted to occur only once, or it can potentially occur multiple times. (Combined, several cardinalities are possible, e.g. 1 , 0-1 , 0-n , 1-n but also 1-2 etc.) Except for some cases that should be self-explanatory, the GUI mostly differentiates between properties that can occur maximum one time, and properties that can occur multiple times. Where only one value is expected, there will only be an input field. Where multiple values can occur, there is an input field, a list of all values and buttons to add and remove values to/from the list. Note: A value is only added to the list, once the "+" button is pressed or enter is hit. For an example, see the image below: While a dataset can only have one title, it may consist of multiple languages.
docs.title: Cardinalities

docs.text: Generally, the value of a property can either be a text-like value (e.g. Text, URLs, etc.), or it can link another object (Dataset, Project, Person, etc.). Text-like values are represented as text fields in the GUI. The value can simply be typed in. Object links are represented as dropdown lists in the GUI. The dropdown shows all objects that can be referenced in this place. The linkable objects can be created and modified in their respective tab. Example: A Project can have either a person or an organization as a point of contact.
docs.title: Values

docs.text: Usage Overview Installation Be sure to have Python 3 installed. Note: The application has only been tested on Python 3.9, but it might work on older versions too. Note: There have been issues with conda installations. If this is the case, consider using a virtual environment. To install the application, run: pip install dsp-metadata-gui Or respectively: pip3 install dsp-metadata-gui Run Once installed, you can start the tool from your terminal: dsp-metadata Collect metadata The application is divided into two main parts: Project Organization and the Form for collecting Metadata . In the Project Organization , you can organize all projects, for which you want to collect metadata. At first, you will need to add a new project. For that you need the project shortcode, which is provided by the DaSCH Client Services. (If you don't have your shortcode yet, get in touch .) After adding at least one project, you can select a project in the list and perform several actions, like editing the data or exporting an RDF serialization of the project metadata. When editing a project the form for collecting metadata opens up. There are multiple tabs, where data can be entered for several classes like: Organization Person Grant Dataset Project Note: The order may seem counter intuitive, not starting from the project and dataset. However, following the order of the tabs is recommended, as e.g. person depends on organization, etc.
docs.title: Usage Overview

In the Project Organization , you can organize all projects, for which you want to collect metadata. At first, you will need to add a new project. For that you need the project shortcode, which is provided by the DaSCH Client Services. (If you don't have your shortcode yet, get in touch .) After adding at least one project, you can select a project in the list and perform several actions, like editing the data or exporting an RDF serialization of the project metadata. When editing a project the form for collecting metadata opens up. There are multiple tabs, where data can be entered for several classes like: Organization Person Grant Dataset Project Note: The order may seem counter intuitive, not starting from the project and dataset. However, following the order of the tabs is recommended, as e.g. person depends on organization, etc.
docs.title: Collect metadata

docs.text: Metadata GUI NB: Due to a bug in python , which causes mkdocstrings to crash on wxPython , this part of the API documentation needs to be maintained by hand. It's therefor not as thorough as the rest of the API documentation. See the source code for clarifications. The GUI Classes The DSP-Metadata-GUI is managed by a set of classes within the module collectMetadata.py and other classes. In this page we introduce the GUI classes. There are the following classes: ProjectFrame ProjectPanel TabOne PropertyRow DataTab HelpPopup TabbedWindow Entry Point collectMetadata () Runner function that launches the app. Calling this method initiates a data handler and opens the GUI. Examples: To run the application, simply call: $ collectMetadata()
docs.title: GUI documentation

docs.text: dataHandling This module holds data handling capabilities for metadatasets. Concretely, the UI has a globally accessible data handler of type DataHandling that will take care of the datasets held by the GUI. DataHandling This class handles data. It checks for availability in the filesystem, and if not, creates the data structure. It also takes care for the storage on disk. The data are stored as a pickle file. The class should not be called as a static. Rather, there should at any given time be an instance of this class ( data_handler ) be available. All calls should be done on this instance, as it holds the actual data representation. add_project ( self , folder_path , shortcode , files ) Add a new project. This project adds a new project folder to the collection after the user specified the folder. The Project is appended at the end of the list. Parameters: Name Type Description Default folder_path str path to the project folder required shortcode str the project shortcode required files list the files in the project folder required export_rdf ( self , path , graph , show = True ) Export RDF serializations to local files. Parameters: Name Type Description Default path str The path where to store the files. required graph Graph The RDF graph of the data. required show bool Flag true, if the folder should be opened after saving the files. Defaults to True. True get_project_by_shortcode ( self , shortcode ) Get the project with a specific shortcode. Parameters: Name Type Description Default shortcode str The shortcode of the project to be found. required Returns: Type Description MetaDataSet MetaDataSet: The Project with said shortcode. import_project ( self , path ) Import a single MetaDataSet from a pickle. Parameters: Name Type Description Default path str path to the pickle to import. required load_data ( self ) Load data from previous runtimes (if any). Currently, this checks ~/DaSCH/config/repos.data . refresh_ui ( self ) Refresh all values in the UI according to the saved values. This method also invokes on-the-fly validation. Note: Calling this method discards all changes that have not been updated in the metaDataSet. remove_project ( self , project ) Removes a specific project. Parameters: Name Type Description Default project MetaDataSet The project to remove. required save_data ( self , dataset = None ) Save data to disc. Currently, the data are stored under ~/DaSCH/config/repos.data . Parameters: Name Type Description Default dataset MetaDataSet A Metadataset to serialize before saving. Defaults to None. None update_all ( self ) Update data according to the values currently in the GUI. Calling this function iterates over each Property in the dataset and updates it with the value found in its corresponding GUI component. validate_and_export_data ( self , index ) Validate a given project and export its RDF data. Parameters: Name Type Description Default index int The index of the MetaDataSet to export. required Returns: Type Description tuple tuple: The result of the validation (see MetaDataSet.validate_graph() ). validate_graph ( self , dataset ) Validates all properties in a specific MetaDataSet. Does not validate each of the properties separately, but rather generates the RDF graph, which then gets validated. Parameters: Name Type Description Default dataset MetaDataSet The dataset to validate. required Returns: Type Description tuple tuple: Validation result (see MetaDataSet.validate_graph() ). zip_and_export ( self , dataset , target ) Zips all data of a project and saves it to a file. The ZIP archive will contain a pickle of the data, all associated files and RDF serializations of the data. Parameters: Name Type Description Default dataset MetaDataSet The dataset to export. required target str The path where to store the export. required
docs.title: Data Handling

docs.text: metaDataSet This module holds the data structure that is used for modelling metadata. The classes defined here aim to represent a metadata-set, closely following the metadata ontology . DataClass Abstract parent class of all classes holding data. add_rdf_to_graph ( self , graph , typename ) Adds the RDF representation of this class to a graph Parameters: Name Type Description Default graph Graph the rdflib.Graph to which the triples should be added required typename str the type name of this class required get_metadataset ( self ) Returns the MetaDataSet to which this class belongs Returns: Type Description MetaDataSet MetaDataSet: The owner MetaDataSet get_properties ( self ) Return a list of all Property fields of this class get_rdf_iri ( self ) Return the IRI of the object. This can be called to use the object as subject of an RDF triple. Returns: Type Description URIRef URIRef: the IRI Dataset Dataset Shape. Corresponds to dsp-repo:Dataset in the ontology. get_properties ( self ) Get all properties held by the object. Returns: Type Description List[Property] A list with all the Properties held by the object. Grant Grant Shape. Corresponds to dsp-repo:Grant in the ontology. get_properties ( self ) Get all properties held by the object. Returns: Type Description List[Property] A list with all the Properties held by the object. MetaDataSet Representation of a dataset. This class represents a dataset of project metadata. It holds the following properties: name: the repo/project name. Typically the name of the folder that was selected. path: the full path of the folder that was selected. files: a list of relevant files in the folder. graph: the RDF graph of the metadata. turtle: the turtle serialization of the overall graph. validation_result: the validation result of the current graph state. project: a metaDataSet.Project representation of the actual metadata (as specified by the ontology). dataset: a list of metaDataSet.Dataset persons: a list of metaDataSet.Person organizations: a list of metaDataSet.Organization __init__ ( self , name , path , shortcode ) special Initiates the object. Parameters: Name Type Description Default name str project name required path str path to the folder associated with the project required shortcode str 4 digit hexadecimal project shortcode required add_dataset ( self ) Add a new dataset add_grant ( self ) Add a new grant add_organization ( self ) Add a new organization add_person ( self ) Add a new person generate_rdf_graph ( self ) Generates the RDF graph of the entire dataset. Returns: Type Description Graph Graph: The RDF graph get_all_properties ( self ) Returns a list of all properties held by fields of this class. (person, dataset, etc.) Returns: Type Description list list[Property]: a List of all properties in this metadataset. get_by_string ( self , s ) Get a data object by its string representation. Checks all available objects for a matching one by its string. Parameters: Name Type Description Default s str string of the object required Returns: Type Description DataClass|None The object matching the string. get_status ( self ) Get the status of the dataset. Indicates if the data is valid according to the SHACL ontology, and how many properties are missing. Returns: Type Description str str: description of the dataset status. get_turtle ( self ) Get the turtle representation of the data. Returns: Type Description str str: a turtle serialization of the data. remove ( self , obj ) remove a DataClass Object Parameters: Name Type Description Default obj Dataclass The object to remove. required validate_graph ( self , graph ) Validates the graph of the entire data against the SHACL ontology. Returns: Type Description tuple tuple: The validation result. Organization Organization Shape. Corresponds to dsp-repo:Organization in the ontology. get_properties ( self ) Get all properties held by the object. Returns: Type Description List[Property] A list with all the Properties held by the object. Person Person Shape. Corresponds to dsp-repo:Person in the ontology. get_properties ( self ) Get all properties held by the object. Returns: Type Description List[Property] A list with all the Properties held by the object. Project Project shape. Corresponds to dsp-repo:Project in our ontology. Parameters: Name Type Description Default name str The name of the project required shortcode str The project shortcode required meta MetaDataSet the owning MetaDataSet required get_properties ( self ) Get all properties held by the object. Returns: Type Description List[Property] A list with all the Properties held by the object. Property General representation of a property. Corresponds to sh:property get_triples ( self , subject ) Returns a Graph containing the triples that represent this property, in respect to a given subject. Parameters: Name Type Description Default subject URIRef the subject to which the property is object required Returns: Type Description Graph Graph: a graph containing one or multiple triples that represent the property get_url_property_id ( self , url ) This method tries to guess the propetyID for a URL. For certain pre-defined cases, a reasonable propertyID is chosen; otherwise, the net location is being extracted, if possible. Parameters: Name Type Description Default url str a URL required Returns: Type Description str str: a propertyID validate ( self ) Validates the current value of the property. Returns: Type Description Tuple[dspMetadataGUI.util.utils.Validity, str] Tuple[Validity, str]: Returns a tuple: First element is a value of the Validity enum, the second one is a human readable validation result.
docs.title: Data Structure

docs.text: utils This module holds utility classes, functions and enums for metadata. are_emails ( mails ) Checks if the list contains only valid e-mail addresses. Parameters: Name Type Description Default mails List[str] A list with (potential) e-mail addresses. required Returns: Type Description bool bool: True, if all strings are valid e-mail addresses; False otherwise. areURLs ( urls ) Checks if a list of strings contains only valid URLs. Parameters: Name Type Description Default urls List[str] a list with (potential) URLs. required Returns: Type Description bool bool: True, if all strings are valid URLs; false otherwise. Cardinality Enumeration of cardinalities that may be used for properties. ONE 1 ONE_TO_TWO 1-2 ONE_TO_UNBOUND 1-n ONE_TO_UNBOUND_ORDERED 1-n ordered UNBOUND 0-n ZERO_OR_ONE 0-1 ZERO_TO_TWO 0-2 Datatype Enumeration of cardinalities that may be used for properties. ADDRESS Postal address ATTRIBUTION Attribution (Role and Agent) CONTROLLED_VOCABULARY Controlled Vocabulary DATA_MANAGEMENT_PLAN Data Management Plan DATE Date (yyyy-mm-dd) DOWNLOAD Download EMAIL E-mail address GRANT Grant (Reference to object) ORGANIZATION Organization (reference to object) PERSON Person (reference to object) PERSON_OR_ORGANIZATION Person or Organization (Reference to object) PLACE Place (represented by URL to authority files) PROJECT Project (reference to object) SHORTCODE Shortcode STRING String literal STRING_OR_URL String or URL URL URL get_coherent_graph ( g ) Gets a coherent subgraph from a graph. That is, if a Property is not connected to the main part of the graph, it will be left out. Parameters: Name Type Description Default g Graph A RDF graph, potentially not coherent. required Returns: Type Description Graph Graph: A coherent subgraph of the graph. get_url_property_id ( url ) This method tries to guess the propetyID for a URL. For certain pre-defined cases, a reasonable propertyID is chosen; otherwise, the net location is being extracted, if possible. Parameters: Name Type Description Default url str a URL required Returns: Type Description str str: a propertyID IRIFactory Factory class to generate unique IRIs. get_unique_iri ( object_type , meta ) classmethod Get a IRI for a object, that is unique to a MetaDataSet. Parameters: Name Type Description Default object_type str Type of the object. required meta MetaDataSet The MetaDataSet to which the IRI should be unique. required Returns: Type Description str str: Unique IRI. is_email ( mail ) Checks, if a string is a valid mail address. Parameters: Name Type Description Default mail str The (potential) e-mail address. required Returns: Type Description bool bool: True, if a valid e-mail address; false otherwise. isURL ( url ) Checks if a string is a valid URL. Parameters: Name Type Description Default url str the (potential) URL string. required Returns: Type Description bool bool: True, if a valid URL; False otherwise. open_file ( path ) Open a file with default application, OS independent. Parameters: Name Type Description Default path str path of the file to open. required Validity Enumeration of validity states. INVALID_VALUE Invalid: The given value is invalid. OPTIONAL_VALUE_MISSING Ignored: No value given on optional field. REQUIRED_VALUE_MISSING Invalid: No value given on required property. UNDEFINED Undefined Value. Should never happen. VALID Valid: The value is valid.

docs.text: Gets a coherent subgraph from a graph. That is, if a Property is not connected to the main part of the graph, it will be left out. Parameters: Name Type Description Default g Graph A RDF graph, potentially not coherent. required Returns: Type Description Graph Graph: A coherent subgraph of the graph.
docs.title: get_coherent_graph()

docs.text: This method tries to guess the propetyID for a URL. For certain pre-defined cases, a reasonable propertyID is chosen; otherwise, the net location is being extracted, if possible. Parameters: Name Type Description Default url str a URL required Returns: Type Description str str: a propertyID
docs.title: get_url_property_id()

docs.text: Factory class to generate unique IRIs.
docs.title: IRIFactory

docs.text: Get a IRI for a object, that is unique to a MetaDataSet. Parameters: Name Type Description Default object_type str Type of the object. required meta MetaDataSet The MetaDataSet to which the IRI should be unique. required Returns: Type Description str str: Unique IRI.
docs.title: get_unique_iri()

docs.text: Checks, if a string is a valid mail address. Parameters: Name Type Description Default mail str The (potential) e-mail address. required Returns: Type Description bool bool: True, if a valid e-mail address; false otherwise.
docs.title: is_email()

docs.text: Checks if a string is a valid URL. Parameters: Name Type Description Default url str the (potential) URL string. required Returns: Type Description bool bool: True, if a valid URL; False otherwise.
docs.title: isURL()

docs.text: Open a file with default application, OS independent. Parameters: Name Type Description Default path str path of the file to open. required
docs.title: open_file()

docs.text: Enumeration of validity states.
docs.title: Validity

docs.text: Invalid: The given value is invalid.
docs.title: INVALID_VALUE

docs.text: Ignored: No value given on optional field.
docs.title: OPTIONAL_VALUE_MISSING

docs.text: Invalid: No value given on required property.
docs.title: REQUIRED_VALUE_MISSING

docs.text: Undefined Value. Should never happen.
docs.title: UNDEFINED

docs.text: Valid: The value is valid.
docs.title: VALID
